stem: Each time a certain component is tested it fails independently with probability \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). If the component is tested [[n]] times, what is the probability that it fails on at least one of the tests?
solution: Use the complement: P(at least one failure) \( = 1 - \) P(no failures) \( = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability of no failures, the complement of the requested event.
This is the probability that every test fails, not at least one.
This adds the per-test probabilities, which double counts overlaps and can exceed the true value.
This subtracts the probability that all tests fail rather than the probability that none do.
This subtracts the sum of per-test probabilities from 1 instead of the 'none' probability.
This is just the single-test failure probability, ignoring that there are [[n]] tests.

stem: Two independent alarm systems monitor a building: system 1 triggers with probability \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and system 2 triggers with probability \( \frac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \) during a break-in. What is the probability that at least one of the two systems triggers?
solution: Use the complement, with independence giving P(neither triggers) as a product: \( P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - (1-P_1)(1-P_2) = 1 - \left(1-\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)\left(1-\frac{[[c]]}{[[d]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(\text{both trigger}) \), the probability of both, not at least one.
This adds the two probabilities, which can double-count the case where both trigger.
This is the probability that NEITHER system triggers, the complement of the requested event.
This subtracts the probability both trigger rather than the probability neither triggers.
This subtracts each probability separately instead of the joint 'neither' probability.
This treats the difference of the probabilities as the answer, which is not a valid probability computation here.

stem: A shipment of [[N]] devices contains [[D]] that are defective. An inspector selects [[k]] devices at random, without replacement, for testing. What is the probability that at least one of the selected devices is defective?
solution: By the complement rule, \( P(\text{at least one defective}) = 1 - P(\text{none defective}) = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{[[N]] - [[D]]}{[[k]]}}{\binom{[[N]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability that NONE of the selected devices are defective, the complement of the requested event.
This is the probability that all [[k]] selected devices are defective, not at least one.
This is the complement of choosing all defectives, not of choosing none defective.
This multiplies the single-device defect rate by [[k]] as if probabilities simply add, ignoring the without-replacement structure.
This incorrectly treats the draws as independent with replacement instead of using the hypergeometric complement.
This is just the single-device probability of being defective, ignoring that [[k]] devices are drawn.

stem: A fair [[m]]-sided die (faces numbered \( 1 \) through [[m]]) is rolled [[k]] times. What is the probability that at least two of the [[k]] rolls show the same number?
solution: By the complement rule, \( P(\text{at least one repeat}) = 1 - P(\text{all [[k]] rolls distinct}) = 1 - \dfrac{[[m]]!/([[m]]-[[k]])!}{[[m]]^{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability that all [[k]] rolls are distinct, the complement of the requested event.
This is the probability that all [[k]] rolls show the exact same fixed number, not merely a repeat.
This is the complement of all rolls matching one fixed number, not the complement of all-distinct.
This omits the \( [[m]]^{[[k]]} \) denominator, leaving an unnormalized count of arrangements.
This treats the probability as if only one pair of rolls could possibly match, ignoring the combinatorics.
This is the fraction of rolls that could repeat, not a valid probability computation for this event.
